local_names.romanized_local: Daun sirih (Malay); Vetrilai (Tamil); Paan ka patta (Hindi); Daun sirih (Indonesian); Bai Plu Sod (Thai)

region_of_origin: South Asia and Southeast Asia (widely used in India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept identified for raw betel leaf as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage is limited to medicinal/toxicological contexts for Piper betle.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of betel leaf is approximately 5 medium leaves (about 15 g), as used in culinary wraps or consumed as a fresh herb accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Betel Leaf, Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for betel leaf, raw (F14010007). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record. Saturated fat and sugar values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11993 / SR Legacy: Betel leaves, raw) where HPB does not report these sub-fractions.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Leafy Greens
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for betel leaf exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Betel leaf contains only 2.04 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 7.1 g dietary fibre, making glycaemic impact negligible. A GI of approximately 15 is estimated by analogy with other non-starchy leafy greens (e.g., spinach, lettuce) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the 10–20 range. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 2.04 / 100) × 15 / 100 = 0.05, confirming negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (682 mg/100 g) relative to moderate sodium (139 mg/100 g), yielding a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Betel leaf provides an excellent potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports vascular tone and blood pressure regulation; consume the leaf alone without areca nut, slaked lime, or other paan additives that introduce additional sodium and harmful alkaloids.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (2.04 g/100 g) and high dietary fibre (7.1 g/100 g) resulting in negligible glycaemic impact; bioactive polyphenols (hydroxychavicol, eugenol) have demonstrated hypoglycaemic activity in preclinical studies
type2_diabetes.guidance: Betel leaf is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load and evidence-based hypoglycaemic phytochemicals; avoid preparations that combine the leaf with sweetened fillings or areca nut, which add sugar and carcinogenic compounds.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (7.1 g/100 g), high potassium (682 mg/100 g), very low saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g), and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Betel leaf consumed as a fresh herb or culinary wrap contributes favourably to cardiovascular health through its high fibre, high potassium, and antioxidant polyphenol content; the leaf must be consumed without areca nut (betel nut), which is independently associated with increased cardiovascular and carcinogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (chavicol, eugenol) and high vitamin C (~70 mg/100 g) as antioxidant support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Betel leaf polyphenols have documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to joint inflammation; do not use as a vehicle for areca nut, which is pro-inflammatory and carcinogenic.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Betel leaf (Piper betle) is a heart-shaped, glossy green leaf from the Piperaceae family, widely cultivated and consumed across South Asia and Southeast Asia, including India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and Thailand. In its raw form, the leaf has a tender yet slightly firm texture with a distinctively peppery, aromatic, and mildly astringent flavour derived from its volatile oils, particularly eugenol and hydroxychavicol. Nutritionally, raw betel leaf is a low-calorie, nutrient-dense leafy vegetable providing 45 kcal per 100 g, with a notable protein content of 4.28 g, very low fat (0.64 g), and minimal net carbohydrate (2.04 g). Its most clinically significant nutritional features are its exceptionally high dietary fibre (7.1 g/100 g), very high potassium (682 mg/100 g), high calcium (275 mg/100 g), significant iron (4.25 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (~70 mg/100 g). The leaf is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load and evidence-based hypoglycaemic phytochemicals. Its favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio supports blood pressure management consistent with DASH dietary principles. Anti-inflammatory polyphenols make it relevant for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Culturally, betel leaf is most commonly associated with the preparation of paan (a traditional South Asian mouth freshener combining the leaf with areca nut, slaked lime, and various fillings), though the leaf itself — consumed without these additives — is nutritionally beneficial. It is also used as a culinary wrap in Thai cuisine (miang kham) and as a fresh herb in various Southeast Asian dishes. The leaf is classified as raw, minimally processed, vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: What are the nutrients in betel leaf?
Is betel leaf good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010007), an approved primary regional source, with exact match to the input record. Saturated fat and sugar sub-fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for betel leaves, raw, which is also an approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for non-starchy leafy greens, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid sub-fraction data for betel leaf not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo (F14010007) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 85–87 g water per 100 g for betel leaves but this value has not been formally adopted here pending direct HPB confirmation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for betel leaf, raw (F14010007); USDA FoodData Central does not carry a complete micronutrient profile for Piper betle. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: betel leaf is a plant food and confirmed to contain no vitamin B12 or vitamin D by formulation. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-derived food, naturally zero cholesterol. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: raw plant leaf, naturally zero industrial trans fat. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is not applicable to a leafy vegetable. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Piper betle exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Vitamin C value of ~70 mg/100 g is referenced from published literature on betel leaf composition (consistent with ICMR-NIN data for similar leafy greens) and is noted as approximate.
